games.title: Off with their Heads!
games.opinion: Overall enjoys the game, especially the sense of discovery in early plays and the cleverness of its trick-taking mechanics where winning isn't always the goal. However, finds it best at 3-4 players, the main strategies can feel linear after several plays, and the poker hand scoring element feels luck-based. The color palette of cards can also be restrictive, and the 'Woods' strategy feels 'all or nothing'.

games.title: Wonderland's War
games.opinion: Mentioned as the world 'Off with their Heads' is set in, an area control bag-building game.

games.title: Title Blades: Barbarian Festival
games.opinion: Another trick-taking game from Judgement City Games with a similar high/low/mid strategy, but the reviewer personally prefers 'Off with their Heads!'.

games.title: Tortuga
games.opinion: A newer trick-taking game from Allplay that focuses on timing wins to get loot in a dungeon.

summary: The video provides a detailed review of the trick-taking game "Off with their Heads!", explaining its gameplay, unique scoring mechanisms across different board sections, and how poker hands are formed. The reviewer discusses the strategic depth of not always needing to win tricks, the evolving value of cards, and the satisfying feeling of executing high-scoring strategies. He also notes some downsides like linearity after multiple plays, luck in poker hands, and a potential "all or nothing" nature of some strategies. The video concludes with the reviewer's final score and recommendations for other trick-taking games.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: Off With Their Heads! Review - Where Would You Like Your Head Cut Off?

games.title: Three Witches
games.opinion: I enjoyed this game, especially the teamwork aspect, the tight bidding for 3 or 4 tricks out of 5, the mathing out of card plays, and the bluffing elements. However, replayability becomes limited once the puzzle is figured out, and sometimes the game can run a bit long. I also have a personal preference for playing as the lead witch.

games.title: The Crew
games.opinion: Recommended for those who appreciate trick-taking with a full team.

games.title: Inside Job
games.opinion: Recommended for those who like the element of team but not team, like a trick-taking game with a social deduction aspect.

games.title: Ghost of Christmas
games.opinion: One of my favorite trick-taking games from Allplay, described as a delightfully good time with a lot of sequencing and very satisfying gameplay.

summary: This video reviews the three-player trick-taking game Three Witches from Allplay, highlighting its unique 1-vs-2 team dynamic, strategic card play, and bluffing opportunities. The reviewer rates the game 3.5 out of 5 (7/10) and discusses its strengths and weaknesses, including its replayability over time. Additional trick-taking game recommendations like The Crew, Inside Job, and Ghost of Christmas are also provided.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: 3 Witches Review - Why Can't We All Just Get Along?

games.title: Giving games to charities/libraries/Goodwill
games.opinion: A feel-good option to get rid of games without monetary return. It's best for lighter, more accessible games and requires checking with the institution (e.g., libraries, youth clubs) to ensure they can use them effectively.

games.title: Selling to Noble Knight Games (bulk buyer)
games.opinion: One of the easiest ways to sell many games at once. You'll receive the least amount of money for your games (around a quarter of their value), as the buyer needs to cover shipping and profit margins.

games.title: Bulk listing on local marketplaces (Craigslist, Facebook Marketplace)
games.opinion: A mid-tier option for getting rid of several games with moderate effort. Offers more money than bulk buyers, especially if you set a price that feels like a very good deal (e.g., selling $450 worth of games for $300).

games.title: Selling through local game stores (events/consignment)
games.opinion: Options vary by store; some may buy games directly, others run events where you sell games for store credit, or offer consignment programs. It requires researching your local stores' policies and events.

games.title: Selling at conventions (used game sales)
games.opinion: Conventions with used game sections (like Origins, Gen Con, Pax Unplugged) can be great if you're within driving distance. Attendees are often eager for deals. Pricing competitively is crucial to attract buyers.

games.title: Selling on BoardGameGeek Marketplace
games.opinion: A good platform to list games individually, reaching a large audience. It requires some effort to list and manage, but can be effective for moving games, especially if offering discounts for multiple purchases.

games.title: Group bartering/giving away within friend circles
games.opinion: A highly favored method, especially within trusted friend groups (e.g., WhatsApp, Discord). You can sell games at a great discount, give them away, or set up a cyclical exchange, fostering community and goodwill.

games.title: Selling on Board Game Exchange (Facebook group)
games.opinion: A large, dedicated Facebook group for buying and selling games, effective for reaching interested buyers. Recommends pricing around 15% below market value for quick sales, and bundling games to save on shipping costs.

games.title: Selling on eBay
games.opinion: Offers wide reach and a 'set it and forget it' convenience. However, it incurs significant fees (PayPal + eBay commissions, around 15%) and shipping costs, making individual sales less profitable. Bulk transactions are harder to set up effectively.

games.title: Trading games on BoardGameGeek
games.opinion: The most preferred method. Allows direct trading of unwanted games for desired ones. It offers maximum value without direct monetary transactions (only shipping costs), especially when doing bulk trades of several games.

summary: The video provides 10 tips on how to effectively get rid of board games, building on a previous video that focused on convincing oneself to declutter. This video focuses on practical methods to sell, trade, or gift games, ranking them from least to most preferred. The host covers options like donating to charities, selling to bulk buyers, using local and online marketplaces, selling at conventions, and trading on specialized platforms. He emphasizes balancing monetary return with effort and convenience, with trading on BoardGameGeek being highlighted as the absolute best method.
_vid_title: 10 Ways To Sell & Trade Your Games

games.title: Earth Siege: Arrival
games.opinion: The game involves one player as the alien invader trying to dominate the board with tokens, while the other player as the human resistance tries to outlast three rounds and prevent alien domination. Players use cards for actions, resource management, and unit deployment. The alien player aims for rapid expansion and increasing the threat level, while the human player focuses on defense and developing technology.

summary: This video is a playthrough of the cooperative board game Earth Siege: Arrival. The hosts, Alex and Professor Meg, explain the basic rules and objectives, with Meg playing as the alien invader aiming for planetary domination and Alex as the human resistance trying to defend Earth. They then proceed to play through the game, demonstrating the core mechanics and player interactions.
_vid_title: Earth Siege: Arrival Gameplay - They're Already Here...

games.title: Earth Siege: Arrival
games.opinion: Highly asymmetric two-player game that feels very thematic, pitting aliens against humans with distinct playstyles and conflicting goals. The reviewer finds the pacing familiar and engaging, with the alien player trying to spread dominance tokens and the human player resisting through combat, research, and threat management.

summary: Professor Meg from the Board Game Co channel previews Earth Siege: Arrival by Plague Island Games, a sponsored preview of a prototype copy for a current crowdfunding campaign. The video covers the game's asymmetric two-player design where one player controls alien invaders and the other leads the human resistance over three rounds. The reviewer explains core mechanics including action and combat card systems, dominance tokens, bases, technology upgrades, salvage tiles, funding cards, threat tracks, delegate tokens, and combat resolution.
classification: new game including how to play
_vid_title: Earth Siege: Arrival Preview - We Love You Carol

games.title: Spectral
games.opinion: A competitive deduction game that he still enjoys but finds it challenging to get his group to play. He prefers solo or cooperative deduction games.

games.title: The Gang
games.opinion: A cooperative poker game that is gateway-friendly and fun. He still considers it a good game but would usually pick 'The Crew' over it, unless playing with non-gamers more familiar with poker.

games.title: Courtesans
games.opinion: A charming, stock-taking-like game with nice components. He loved it when he first got it and played it a lot, but over time it has seen less play and has shrunk to the background of his collection.

games.title: Ironwood
games.opinion: A delightful asymmetric two-player experience with solo modes. It's an excellent game with great card play that he still owns and likes, but finds two-player games in the 45-minute to 1-hour range harder to get to the table.

games.title: Tír na nÓg
games.opinion: An excellent point optimization game with open table drafting and an interesting puzzle involving piece placement and card claiming. While he enjoys this genre, he tends to default to older favorites like Calico and Cascadia, causing this one to fade slightly.

games.title: Galactic Cruise
games.opinion: An intricate and highly rewarding Euro game. He still considers it excellent, but over time, it has started to feel a bit more like 'work' or a 'sequence' to follow rather than a personally engaging engine-building experience. He's questioning if he needs it personally.

games.title: Things in Rings
games.opinion: An excellent deduction puzzle where players try to figure out hidden rules. It was a 5/5 game for him initially and he still loves and plays it regularly. However, the initial intense excitement has lessened, moving from a must-play to a game he's happy to pull out.

games.title: Harmonies
games.opinion: A very good point-scoring game with charming chunky bits. Despite being considered better than Cascadia by some, he still prefers to play Cascadia due to similar vibes, causing Harmonies to be reached for less often.

games.title: Arydia: The Paths We Dare Tread
games.opinion: The gameplay experience is top-notch, one of the best campaign games he's played, but its campaign nature makes it difficult to re-engage with after a break, as it's hard to remember where things left off.

games.title: Slay the Spire: The Board Game
games.opinion: An incredible game that he loves and owns. However, most of his plays are solo, and the excellent digital video game adaptation makes him often default to playing it digitally to avoid the physical setup and teardown.

summary: The video discusses the top 10 board games released in 2024 that have 'faded' for the reviewer over the past year or year and a half. He clarifies that 'fading' doesn't mean the games are bad; many are still excellent, owned, and recommended, but the initial excitement or play frequency has decreased for personal reasons.
_vid_title: Top 10 Games from 2024 That Faded for me

games.title: Heroes of the Shire: Light and Shadow
games.opinion: The game offers multiple ways to play, including solo/co-op scenario mode and a competitive arena mode. Players build and level up heroes, gaining unique spells and abilities. Scenarios can be 'light' or 'shadow,' impacting bosses and actions. Combat is strategic with no luck involved, focusing on cooldown management and ability usage. There are unique characters with distinct abilities and branching pathways for progression. The game also features diverse enemies, mini-bosses, and exploration mechanics. Difficulty can be adjusted through modification cards.

summary: This video provides a comprehensive preview of 'Heroes of the Shire: Light and Shadow,' a board game currently running a Gamefound campaign. The reviewer explains the core gameplay mechanics, including character progression, spellcasting, combat, and exploration in both solo/co-op scenario mode and a competitive arena mode. She details player boards, enemy types, and how turns and encounters unfold on modular hex maps. The video emphasizes the strategic depth and unique hero abilities without relying on dice rolls for combat outcomes, using dice only for tracking cooldowns and health.
classification: new game including how to play
_vid_title: Heroes of the Shire: Light & Shadow Preview - Enjoy Cooling Down!

games.title: 12 Rivers
games.opinion: A fun and charming game with a high toy factor and great table presence. The villager abilities are fun and offer interesting optimization. However, token powers are often underwhelming or imbalanced, and discarding cards for economy rather than their abilities can feel less engaging. The game has low replayability, with the experience becoming less interesting after multiple plays due to a lack of variety in goals and gameplay, despite the initial appeal of its marble-rolling mechanic.

games.title: Potion Explosion
games.opinion: Recommended as another game with a 'toy factor' involving marbles exploding out of a chute, offering combined abilities.

games.title: Fireball Island
games.opinion: Recommended for a 'completely chaotic' experience that also uses marbles with a high toy factor and significant randomness.

games.title: River Valley Glassworks
games.opinion: Recommended as a game with a similar charming weight factor, slightly more strategic depth, and strong component factor, though with less of a toy factor.

summary: The video provides a detailed review of the board game '12 Rivers,' including a breakdown of its gameplay mechanics such as scoring through villagers and marble collection. The reviewer highlights the game's high 'toy factor' and engaging player interaction but notes some drawbacks concerning unbalanced abilities and declining replayability. The video concludes with a score for '12 Rivers' and recommendations for other marble-themed board games.
_vid_title: 12 Rivers Review - Stop, Drop & Score Your Way To The Top

games.title: High Society
games.opinion: A classic, tense bidding game with great new cards in the All Play version, but held back by frequent unintentional kingmaking that can lead to frustrating experiences.

games.title: Modern Art
games.opinion: Recommended as an alternative bidding/auction game where players must be mindful of money and how well it is spent.

games.title: Ra
games.opinion: Considered possibly one of the best pure auction games out there, slightly longer than a filler but worth checking out.

games.title: Money
games.opinion: A similar auction game by the same designer as High Society; reviewer considers High Society the better of the two, though both are good.

games.title: QE
games.opinion: Recommended as a chaotic bidding game from All Play that breaks conventional bidding rules while still penalizing the biggest spender.

summary: Alex Radcliffe from Board Game Co reviews High Society by All Play, a 3-5 player auction game where players bid on status cards to gain points while avoiding being the player with the least money. The review covers gameplay mechanics, the new cards added in the All Play edition, and concludes with a 3.5/5 rating, praising the game's tension and clean design but noting unintentional kingmaking as its main flaw.
_vid_title: High Society Review - Status Is Acquirable...Just Don't Go Broke

games.title: Aquaria
games.opinion: Everything in the game works together well, but nothing individually shines. The dial mechanics feel like busywork, and the exploration board isn't particularly rewarding. Early game can feel like a lot of effort for small progress, and end game can become overly mathematical. Despite these points, chaining actions and filling the grid can be satisfying. The game features multiple synergistic systems. Overall, the reviewer enjoyed his plays but it didn't 'click' in a magical way.

games.title: Emberheart
games.opinion: Recommended for those looking for another game of collecting a variety of creatures with worker placement puzzles.

games.title: Finsband
games.opinion: Recommended for those looking for a different kind of fish engine and tableau building.

games.title: The Networks
games.opinion: Recommended as another card-building tableau collecting game.

summary: This video is a rapid-fire review of the board game Aquaria. The reviewer, Alex Radcliffe, provides a brief overview of the game's mechanics, including actions powered by pearls, placing fish cards into an aquarium grid, rotating dials for bonuses, and moving on an exploration board. He shares his subjective opinion on the game, noting that while it is well-designed and functional, it lacks a standout element that makes it truly engaging for him. He also offers recommendations for other games with similar themes or mechanics.
_vid_title: Aquaria Rapid Fire Review - Those Fish Want To Stick Together!

games.title: The Glasgow Train Robbery
games.opinion: A two-player cooperative game based on a true story, with top-notch art and production. The reviewer found the beginning of the game to be very slow-paced with little threat, with the real tension and fun moments occurring primarily towards the end. The game feels like a lot of setup for the final conclusion, which is thematically appropriate but not as engaging throughout from a gameplay perspective.

games.title: Burgle Bros
games.opinion: Recommended as a cooperative/solo game for pulling off a heist with a different gameplay style.

games.title: Core of Discovery Duo
games.opinion: Recommended for its hidden communication tension and working with a partner, without the heist theme.

summary: This video is a rapid-fire review of The Glasgow Train Robbery, a two-player cooperative game based on a true historical event. The reviewer provides an overview of the game's objective to execute a five-step heist plan with limited communication between players, utilizing agents and tools on the board while managing a ticking train timer and unexpected contingencies. The reviewer then shares their thoughts on the game's pacing, tension, and production, ultimately giving it a score and recommending other similar cooperative games.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: The Glasgow Train Robbery Review - We Need More Batteries!

games.title: One Piece: Dawn of Liberation
games.opinion: The hosts appear to enjoy the game's thematic depth and mechanical variety, appreciating the escalating pirate threat, cooperative mission structure, character upgrade system, and boss encounters. They note it is a detailed and engaging cooperative experience especially for One Piece fans, with a satisfying progression across acts.

summary: Alex Radical and Professor Mac from Board Game Cow play through Act One of One Piece: Dawn of Liberation by Kesco, a sponsored cooperative board game for 1-4 players. They walk viewers through the game's core mechanics including the Kaido wheel, pirate dice escalation, captain and crew actions, stash missions, samurai recruitment, boss battles, and the red scabbard system, concluding with the completion of Act One and a brief overview of what Act Two and the final boss encounter entail.
_vid_title: One Piece: Dawn of Liberation Gameplay - The Pirates Are Overwhelming!

games.title: Kaelora
games.opinion: A charming, accessible set collection and push-your-luck game with a lot to juggle in scoring. The moon token and luck-based region scoring are weak points, but overall a fun experience with a distinct solo mode. Rated 3.5 out of 5.

games.title: Pteranova
games.opinion: Recommended as a related game for fans of closing off a board; reviewer found it very fun, though it differs from Kaelora in that area control is less direct.

games.title: Azul
games.opinion: Mentioned as a comparable game due to its shared board competition and grid placement mechanics, which feel similar to Kaelora.

games.title: Waddle
games.opinion: Mentioned as a sponsored recommendation; described as a cutthroat area control game where players score by surrounding zones with penguin tokens.

summary: Alex Rad from Board Gameco reviews Kaelora by Tangerine Games, a 1-4 player set collection and push-your-luck game where players move an explorer across a shared board to collect elemental and animal tokens, placing them on a personal grid to score points through matching rows, animal majority scoring, area control via tents, and bonus objective cards. The review covers gameplay mechanics, likes (depth of decisions, push-your-luck, solo mode variety), dislikes (moon token frustration, luck in region scoring, berry scoring feeling anticlimactic), and concludes with a 3.5/5 rating along with recommendations for Pteranova, Azul, and Waddle.
_vid_title: Kaelora Review - 1, 2, 3 and Score!

summary: Alex Radler from Board Game Co. gives a final call announcement for Level Up Retreat 2026, a board game playing convention taking place March 20-22 in Woodbridge, New Jersey. He discusses attendance limits (400 tickets, ~75 remaining), the event's focus on playing games and meeting people, and provides lists of hot games and play-to-win games that attendees can expect at the event, while noting the lists may change slightly before the event.
_vid_title: Level Up Retreat 2026 Final Call - Last Details, Hot Games, Play To Wins & More!

games.title: Torchlit
games.opinion: A thinky and rewarding trick-taking game with a dungeon-crawl theme that feels different in a crowded space. Enjoys the strategy and mechanics like matching advancement and lowest-card agency, but finds the chaos/luck factor can undermine careful planning, especially in the final trick.

games.title: Cat in the Box
games.opinion: Recommended as a more accessible but still thinky and creative trick-taking game; one of the reviewer's favorite trick-taking games.

games.title: Origin Story
games.opinion: Recommended as a trick-taking game with a unique theme similar to Torchlit, where you build up powers as you play.

games.title: Runes
games.opinion: A hand-shedding game (not trick-taking) from AllPlay with similar vibes to Torchlit — lots of strategy and a sense of control mixed with chaos.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Gameco reviews Torchlit, a trick-taking dungeon-crawl game from AllPlay designed by David Spolinsky. The video includes a walkthrough of how the game is played, covering bidding, trick resolution, dungeon advancement via winning or matching, loot distribution, and the final-trick swap mechanic, followed by a review covering production quality, strategic depth, and the chaotic elements. The game receives a 4 out of 5 rating. Additional recommendations include Cat in the Box, Origin Story, and Runes.
_vid_title: Torchlit Review - Can You Time That Perfect Loot Grab?

games.title: Honeypot
games.opinion: A fun and charming bluffing/mind game that does a good job of being distinct within its genre. Best at 4+ players. Main complaints are not enough cards per player by game end, a catch-up mechanic that feels overtuned and doesn't always target the actual last-place player, and stealing actions feeling more blind than envelope construction. Overall a good time for fans of bluffing and mind games, but not a high-strategy game — more of a party game with a bluffing element.

games.title: Agent Avenue
games.opinion: Recommended as a similar mind game where players try to figure out whether opponents can read their intentions.

games.title: Skull
games.opinion: One of the reviewer's favorite games in the bluffing genre, highly recommended for fans of mind games.

games.title: Turnip
games.opinion: Recommended as another mind game with a bluffing element from Rose Gauntlet Entertainment, mentioned as a channel sponsor tie-in.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Game Co reviews Honeypot, a 1-6 player bluffing and mind game from Flatout Games currently in prototype/crowdfunding. Players construct envelopes of cards to tempt or deter opponents, who reveal cards two at a time and decide whether to take them or go deeper. The review covers game mechanics, card scoring types, the steal mechanic, and gives a 3.5/5 rating, praising its player-reading dynamics while criticizing low card counts and an overpowered catch-up mechanic. Agent Avenue, Skull, and Turnip are recommended as similar games.
_vid_title: Honeypot Review - Pay No Attention to the Cards Behind the....Cards

games.title: Lords of Waterdeep
games.opinion: Renegade is doing a reprint with no significant changes mentioned.

games.title: Citadels Duo
games.opinion: Reviewer is skeptical it needs a two-player adaptation but is curious to see if they do it justice.

games.title: Gateway
games.opinion: Enjoyed it more than expected from reading the rules. Found intricate decisions in this area control deck building game. Gave 3.5/5 tentatively, thinks it could be a 4 with more final content.

games.title: Foes of Middle Earth
games.opinion: Wanted to like it but found it reminiscent of Cthulhu Death May Die but worse, making it hard to justify over that game.

games.title: Cthulhu Death May Die
games.opinion: Loves this game and considers it the superior alternative to Foes of Middle Earth.

games.title: Smitten
games.opinion: Fun, charming puzzle enjoyed for cooperative and solo play, though not sure it's a forever game.

games.title: Smitten 2
games.opinion: Fun, charming puzzle enjoyed for cooperative and solo play, though not sure it's a forever game.

games.title: Soore
games.opinion: Charming little surprise. Enjoyed the gameplay and the strategic unfolding. Tentatively at least 3.5, probably a 4, but unsure if it would stay in the collection.

games.title: Fractured Sky
games.opinion: Enjoyed the expansions a lot. Recommends the game and its expansions, but City of Spies Assault remains the go-to in that genre for the reviewer.

games.title: City of Spies: Estoril 1942
games.opinion: One of the reviewer's favorite games in the mind-think placement genre, always beats out similar games like Fractured Sky.

games.title: One Piece
games.opinion: Decent and charming but felt way too easy with no sense of challenge. Rated a 3 out of 5 due to lack of difficulty, though balance issues may be prototype-related.

games.title: Ashes and Amber
games.opinion: Sponsored content covered on the channel but reviewer did not play it and cannot offer personal thoughts.

games.title: Legions of Cadmon
games.opinion: Felt prototypey and unpolished with some fun satisfying moments. Tentative rating of 3 out of 5.

games.title: Dr. Science
games.opinion: Solid tableau-building trick-taking game rated 3.5/5. Clever design but the hook didn't pull the reviewer in compared to other trick-taker games.

games.title: Nvidum
games.opinion: Asymmetric deck-building game. Solo mode tentatively a 4/5 but only one scenario played. Competitive mode less enjoyed due to information overload and genre not being a fit for the reviewer.

games.title: Whisp
games.opinion: Still enjoying it after multiple plays. Criticism remains that it can incentivize pursuing the same strategy repeatedly, but overall still a positive experience.

games.title: Ember
games.opinion: Still fun after a few months away. Rates it 4/5. A satisfying puzzle with moments of feeling stuck trying to figure out combos.

games.title: Ticket to Ride Legacy
games.opinion: 5/5 game. Finished the full campaign a second time, this time as a four-player experience which was enjoyed more than two-player. Would happily play again in a few years.

games.title: Path of Civilization
games.opinion: Enjoyed it enough to want another play but doesn't feel the need to keep it. Prefers Age of Civilization as more clever, condensed, and satisfying.

games.title: Age of Civilization
games.opinion: Would recommend it easily over Path of Civilization for being more clever, condensed, and satisfying with less game time.

games.title: Torchlet
games.opinion: Review going up on the channel today; no detailed opinion shared in this video.

games.title: Honeypot
games.opinion: Review going up on the channel today as part of a multi-crowdfunding campaign from Flat Games; no detailed opinion shared in this video.

games.title: Tulico
games.opinion: Rules read and ready. Looks like a fun little optimization puzzle but reviewer is skeptical it will be a keeper.

games.title: Alspire
games.opinion: A slightly bigger cooperative 3D game from Faceship Games. Reviewer is intrigued but hasn't read the rules yet and hopes to play before Gamma.

summary: Alex Wack from Board Game Co presents a news and week-in-review video covering recent board game announcements (including a Lords of Waterdeep reprint, Citadels Duo, a new Lord of the Rings game, and a Cichlides expansion), recaps videos published during the week (reviews, previews, and gameplays for Gateway, Foes of Middle Earth, Smitten, Soore, Fractured Sky, One Piece, Ashes and Amber, Legions of Cadmon, Dr. Science, and Nvidum), shares games played personally (Whisp, Ember, Ticket to Ride Legacy, Path of Civilization), and previews upcoming content while noting a lighter schedule due to preparing for Gamma Trade Expo.
_vid_title: News & Week In Review - A New Lord of the Rings Game, Citadels Duo & More!!!

games.title: Casinopolis
games.opinion: Still in collection, loves the whole Agropolis/Sprawlopolis series. Tiny wallets, easy to keep, enjoys them all.

games.title: Massive Darkness: Dungeons of Shadow Reach
games.opinion: Still excited for it but not yet received. The base Massive Darkness game is kept but questioned given overlap with Cthulhu Death May Die and Zombicide.

games.title: An Age Contrived
games.opinion: Rated 4/5, liked it a lot but it did not survive the cull to 200 games. Teaching new players is a barrier and a 4/5 game needs fewer barriers to justify keeping.

games.title: Link City
games.opinion: Still in collection, fantastic party-style word association game. Highly recommended.

games.title: Caper: Europe
games.opinion: Still here, solid 4/5 two-player game with few barriers. However, under consideration for culling when comparing against other two-player games in a 200-game limit.

games.title: Ethnos
games.opinion: Appreciated the new Stonemaier version's graphic design and art, but it did not survive the cull down to 200 games.

games.title: Point Galaxy
games.opinion: Liked the prototype, found it fun and satisfying, but it did not make it into the collection due to strict 200-game limit.

games.title: Daybreak
games.opinion: Loved it initially, played ~15 times. Really good but somewhat repetitive. Gets plays on Board Game Arena now; did not survive the cull to 200 games.

games.title: Arydia: The Paths We Dare Tread
games.opinion: Rates it 4.5/5, solid experience, highly recommended. However, it's a campaign game that feels like homework to return to after a break, so it didn't work for his play style.

games.title: Cloak Cats
games.opinion: Still in collection 5 years later. Kids still enjoy it and it still gets played.

games.title: Tapestry
games.opinion: Has cycled in and out of collection multiple times. Currently kept because expansions aren't on Board Game Arena. Solid 4/5 but table time is a concern and it's likely to be culled eventually.

games.title: Kingdom Rush: Elemental Uprising
games.opinion: Still here, loves it. Unique tower defense plus polyomino combination that really works. Wants to complete the solo campaign this year.

games.title: Bloodstone
games.opinion: Covered the prototype 5 years ago from Druid City Games. Still excited for it if it ever launches, but it still hasn't arrived.

games.title: Truffle Shuffle
games.opinion: Still in collection. Simple chocolate-drafting game from AEG. Doesn't wow him but the kids like it and it fits as a family game.

games.title: V-Commandos
games.opinion: Eventually got rid of it. Loved it, but traded it out for Assassin's Creed, which he also eventually got rid of. Sad given Triton Noir shut down.

games.title: Ragnarok
games.opinion: Still in collection 5 years later. Loves the two-player abstract area control with player powers, similar in feel to Santorini. Fantastic experience.

summary: In this 'Where Are They Now' episode for February 2026, Alex from Board Game Co. revisits games he covered in February 2025 and February 2021 to see how they've fared over time. He has adjusted the format to focus only on games with meaningful updates—things kept, culled, or debated—rather than games he simply never revisited. Key themes include his effort to limit his collection to 200 games, the impact of teachability and replayability on keeping games, and how campaign games that are hard to return to don't fit his play style.
_vid_title: Where Are They Now? - February 2026: Arydia, Massive Darkness, Tapestry & More!!

games.title: Lord of the Rings: Foes of Middle Earth
games.opinion: The hosts enjoyed the game overall but noted it started feeling deceptively easy before becoming quite punishing, especially due to poor health management heading into the boss fight. They found it a fairly straightforward cooperative experience in a positive sense, with thematic character choices and fun mechanics, though admitted to some rules mistakes during play.

summary: Alex and Professor M play through a sponsored prototype of Lord of the Rings: Foes of Middle Earth, a cooperative board game. They navigate three phases: an exploratory journey to the Prancing Pony, a boss fight against the Watcher in the Water, and a final Balrog encounter. They ultimately lose during the Watcher in the Water phase due to poor health management, and direct viewers to other playthroughs for the Balrog phase. The video includes a note that it is a prototype and links to the crowdfunding campaign.
_vid_title: Lord of the Rings: Foes of Middle Earth Preview - It Seemed So Peaceful...At First

games.title: Dr. Science
games.opinion: An interesting and clever tableau-building trick-taking hybrid that does something new, but lacks the wow factor to make it truly stand out. Enjoyable and strategically sound, but hasn't resonated deeply on a personal level.

games.title: Cat in the Box
games.opinion: One of the reviewer's favorite trick-taking games, praised highly for its clever and interesting twist on the genre.

games.title: Rummy
games.opinion: Recommended for players who enjoy set-building and reconfiguring hands, noted as a parallel to Dr. Science's tableau mechanics.

games.title: Ghost of Christmas
games.opinion: One of the reviewer's favorite trick-taking games and one of the first that got them into the genre when they previously didn't enjoy trick-taking.

summary: Alex Rabbit from Board Gameco reviews Dr. Science by Crosscut Games, a prototype tableau-building trick-taking game. The video explains the rules through a partial playthrough and concludes with a review, giving it a 3.5 out of 5. The reviewer appreciates its unique blend of rummy-style tableau building with trick-taking but notes it lacks a personal wow factor.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: Dr. Science Review - Tableaus & Tricks

games.title: Cloud War Tales: Nuvitrum
games.opinion: An asymmetric deck-building card skirmish game that the reviewer enjoyed more in solo mode than competitively. Main criticisms include heavy iconography making it hard to read opponent strategies, and a general disconnect with the cards-on-a-board skirmish genre. Praised for asymmetric factions, scenario-based play, and depth for dedicated players.

games.title: Summoner Wars Master Set
games.opinion: Recommended as a similar game for those interested in competitive cards-on-a-board army building and head-to-head play.

games.title: Call of Killforth
games.opinion: Recommended as a similar game for those interested in solo or competitive cards-on-a-board gameplay.

games.title: Resist
games.opinion: Briefly mentioned as a sponsored recommendation for a solo card battler deck-building experience from Salt and Pepper Games.

summary: Alex Radcliff from Board Gameco provides a rapid-fire review of the prototype for Cloud War Tales: Nuvitrum by Piff Games, an asymmetric deck-building card skirmish game played on a board. He covers the game's mechanics, asymmetric factions, scenario-based play, solo and competitive modes, and gives it a 3.5 out of 5, noting he enjoyed solo play more than competitive due to difficulty reading opponent strategies from heavily iconographic cards.
_vid_title: Cloud War Tales: Nuvitrum Rapid Fire Review - Asymmetrical Deck Building Grid Warfare

games.title: Metal Gear Solid (board game)
games.opinion: Started the first two tutorial missions but never finished it; needs to review it at some point.

games.title: Zia: Legend of the Rift System (solo campaign)
games.opinion: One of the solo legacy-adjacent games he is considering committing to and finishing in 2026; was leaning towards this one.

games.title: Santorini: Sphinx
games.opinion: Started but never finished; one of the solo campaign options he wants to complete.

games.title: Kingdom Rush: Elemental Uprising
games.opinion: Tempting solo campaign option he is considering committing to in 2026.

games.title: Sky Tier Horde
games.opinion: Mentioned as one of the legacy or campaign-adjacent solo board games he may commit to finishing.

summary: Alex from Board Game Co shares his 10 personal resolutions for 2026, covering a wide range of topics including playing 30 out of 35 games on his challenge list, getting off Facebook and Reels, reducing his review backlog to under 100 games, maintaining his diet and weight, finishing a major video game, finishing painting his house, completing a solo legacy board game campaign, producing valuable reports for Gamefound, learning 365 new games across the year, and breaking the habit of snoozing through multiple alarms.
_vid_title: 2026 New Years Resolutions - Board Games, Health, Social Media & More!!

games.title: Ashes & Amber
games.opinion: A thematic civilization-building card game that escalates through historical ages, with engaging mechanics around city building, catastrophe mitigation, and card management. The reviewer finds it thematically immersive and strategically rewarding, especially in how players must maneuver cards and protect their cities.

summary: Professor Meg from the Board Game Co channel presents a sponsored preview of Ashes & Amber by David's Games, a prototype civilization-building card game currently on crowdfunding. The video covers game setup, round structure, card types, city-building mechanics, catastrophes, resilience cards, wonders, accomplishments, death cards, and how the game escalates across five historical ages from prehistory to the modern era.
classification: new game including how to play
_vid_title: Ashes & Amber - Cities Will Rise...As Will The Cards

games.title: Smitten
games.opinion: A cute, puzzly card game that is simple and accessible but not amazing. Slight preference for this one over Smitten 2 due to clearer rules and more natural card abilities. Can sometimes come down to luck of the draw rather than pure optimization. Enjoyable but not a forever game.

games.title: Smitten 2
games.opinion: Similar to Smitten but with slightly more confusing card text and fiddlier abilities. Comes in a nicer box. Reviewer found the rules less immediately clear than the original. Still enjoyable but slightly less preferred than the first game.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Gameco plays through and reviews both Smitten and Smitten 2 in solo mode on a Solo Sunday video. He attempts multiple playthroughs of each game, losing some and winning one, while explaining the rules and mechanics. Both games receive a 3.5 out of 5 rating, praised for being simple and accessible but noted for occasionally coming down to luck rather than skill.
_vid_title: Smitten & Smitten 2 Playthrough & Review - Can I Play 9 Cards Successfully?

games.title: Forage
games.opinion: Enjoyable roll-and-write with satisfying end-game combos, but the first two-thirds of the game feels slow and unrewarding, making it hard to feel like progress is being made until the final rounds when everything chains together.

games.title: That's Pretty Clever
games.opinion: Recommended as a similar combo-chaining game where the reviewer feels like they are getting something every turn, with a better overall arc than Forage.

games.title: Cascadia Rolling Rivers
games.opinion: Enjoyed as a roll-and-write version of the Cascadia system from Flatout Games.

games.title: Cascadia Rolling Hills
games.opinion: Enjoyed as a roll-and-write version of the Cascadia system from Flatout Games.

games.title: Cascadero
games.opinion: Mentioned as a smaller, accessible roll-and-write version of Cascadero from Bitewing Games with similar dice-gathering and chaining mechanics.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Gameco reviews Forage, a 1-6 player roll-and-write game from Flatout Games, demonstrating gameplay across several turns and explaining the core mechanics of dice drafting, card types (pantry, exploration, gift-giving), time management, and combo chaining. The reviewer gives it a 3.5 out of 5, praising the satisfying end-game combos but criticizing the slow, unrewarding feel of the first two-thirds of the game. The video also briefly recommends similar games including That's Pretty Clever, Cascadia Rolling Rivers, Cascadia Rolling Hills, and Cascadero.
_vid_title: Forage Review - Explore, Store & Gift Your Way Through The Dice

games.title: Labyrinth Chronicles
games.opinion: A charming, addictive, and accessible cooperative progression game with excellent production quality, but ultimately too light and lacking in challenge for experienced gamers. The unlocking system is engaging and gave the reviewer a zen-like solo experience, but some stalled progression moments and the lack of a true loss condition hold it back.

games.title: Mythwind
games.opinion: Recommended as a similar progression-based game where you cannot really lose but engage with content.

games.title: Dwar7s Romantic
games.opinion: Mentioned as a comparable experience where you unlock content at different paces depending on performance rather than truly losing.

games.title: Sail Legacy
games.opinion: Recommended as a cooperative legacy-light game with unlocking content, branching elements, and replayability for fans of Labyrinth Chronicles.

summary: Alex Radcliff from Board Game Co. reviews Labyrinth Chronicles, a cooperative progressive campaign game from Awaken Realms and Ravensburger built on the classic Labyrinth. The review covers gameplay mechanics, the unlocking progression system, production quality, and target audience considerations, concluding with a score of 3.5 out of 5 for its charming but light experience.
_vid_title: Labyrinth Chronicles Review - It's Pretty, It's Fun....But Is It Challenging Enough?

games.title: Thief's Market
games.opinion: Pleasantly surprised by it, enjoyed the clever dice-splitting mechanic and tableau building, but it plays the same every time and is best as a light beer-and-pretzels filler game rather than a repeat play experience.

games.title: Cash and Guns
games.opinion: Recommended as a chaotic alternative for splitting loot.

games.title: Good Critters
games.opinion: Recommended for its fun loot-splitting arc, believed to be from Arcane Wonders.

games.title: Soda Jerk
games.opinion: A very different game but gave similar vibes to Thief's Market in terms of the shared economy and collective elevating or tanking of scoring.

summary: Alex Radcliff from Board Gameco reviews Thief's Market by Piggy Pocket Games, a 3-5 player loot-splitting game. He explains the mechanics of dice rolling, pile creation, and stealing, then gives his thoughts: he enjoyed it more than expected, praising the I-cut-you-choose puzzle and tableau building, but notes it feels repetitive across plays and is best as a light filler. He rates it 3.5 out of 5 and recommends Cash and Guns, Good Critters, and Soda Jerk as related games.
_vid_title: Thief's Market Review - But I Want All Of It...

games.title: Viticulture: Bordeaux
games.opinion: A fun new board for Viticulture that introduces expert spots as the standout mechanic, but is a nice-to-have rather than a must-have, especially if you already own Tuscany. The static roster of experts is a minor drawback, and the expansion nudges players toward wine-making as a more central strategy.

games.title: Viticulture
games.opinion: Referenced as the base game; the reviewer enjoys it and uses it as the benchmark for evaluating the Bordeaux expansion.

games.title: Viticulture: Tuscany
games.opinion: Referenced as a comparable expansion; the reviewer particularly likes the star chart in Tuscany and considers it a prime Viticulture experience, suggesting Bordeaux is less essential if you already own Tuscany.

summary: Alex from Board Gameco reviews the Bordeaux expansion for Viticulture by Stonemaier Games. The expansion provides a new board with changes including face-up card decks for vines and orders, a personal trade board, a turn order chart with bonuses, and most notably an expert system where players claim unique permanent ability spots. The reviewer scores it 3.5 out of 5, calling it a fun but non-essential addition, especially for those who already own Tuscany.
_vid_title: Viticulture: Bordeaux Expansion Review - A New Board!!(eaux)

games.title: Bohemians
games.opinion: Won awards but player feedback is less positive. Intrigued by the art and Portal Games pedigree, but the 6.6 BGG rating has dampened excitement. Deck-building game with a life journey theme.

games.title: Irish Gauge
games.opinion: A lighter, accessible train/investment game from 2014 that the reviewer missed when it first came out. The 7.2 rating lowers expectations but it's quick and seen as a good intro to the 18X genre.

games.title: Path of Civilization
games.opinion: A civilization-building game the reviewer wanted to play when it first came out but missed. Still intrigued despite a 7.2 BGG rating. Now wants to try it on BGA.

games.title: The Game Makers
games.opinion: A meta game about the board game hobby from Bezio Games that raised over a million in crowdfunding. A 7.0 rating and skepticism about meta-style games tempers excitement, but the theme and worker placement mechanics are interesting enough to try on BGA.

games.title: Teranova
games.opinion: Part of the Terra Mystica family of games. Reviewer likes but never loved Terra Mystica or Gaia Project. Familiarity with the system makes it an easy learn and worth trying to see if it clicks.

games.title: In the Year of the Dragon
games.opinion: A Stefan Feld game the reviewer has long wanted to play but never tabled. Got rid of the physical copy. Intrigued by the disaster mechanics typical of Feld designs. Has never disliked a Feld game, just a question of how much they'll like it.

games.title: Tower Up
games.opinion: Had early buzz at conventions but excitement faded over time. Seen as potentially having a light simplicity that makes for an engaging experience. Has been waiting to try before buying. Now available on BGA, making it a good time to check it out.

games.title: Praha Caput Regni
games.opinion: Has owned it for a long time but never played it. Rated much higher than Evacuation (which they enjoyed). A 7.8 and top 200 BGG ranking signal it's likely a great game. The chaining action bonuses are a particularly compelling design feature.

games.title: Yokohama
games.opinion: Played once before and found it pretty good but not wow-worthy. Top 150 on BGG at a 7.8. Wants to give it another chance with the newer version, hoping additional plays will spark more enthusiasm, though acknowledges it may just be the same experience.

games.title: Keyflower
games.opinion: Played once 4-5 years ago and remembered it as absolutely brilliant. Has kept it in the collection based on that single play alone. Thinks the production looks terrible and wishes for a deluxe version. Excited to relearn and replay it on BGA, with the caveat that it might not hold up to memory.

summary: Alex Radcliff from Board Game Co presents 10 Board Game Arena games he wants to play in 2026 as part of a broader 35-game personal challenge. The video ranks each game from 10 to 1 based on his excitement level, discussing BGG ratings, personal history with each title, and why he wants to try them. The list includes a mix of games he has never played and two (Yokohama and Keyflower) he has played once before and wants to revisit.
_vid_title: 10 BoardGameArena Games I Want To Knock Off My List in 2026

games.title: Piñatas
games.opinion: A fun but basic trick-taking game with a satisfying timing mechanic around when you go out, but lacks longevity and variety compared to other trick-taking games on the market. Rated 3.5 out of 5.

games.title: Rebel Princess
games.opinion: Recommended as a superior alternative that constantly shifts the formula each round, providing more variety than Piñatas.

games.title: Skull King
games.opinion: Recommended as another trick-taking game with strong timing elements and special cards that keep things interesting.

summary: Alex Radcliff from Board Gameco reviews Piñatas by All Play, a 2-5 player trick-taking game. The video covers the rules and mechanics, focusing on the core hook of timing when you go out to maximize points. The reviewer gives it a 3.5 out of 5, praising the timing mechanism but noting it is a one-trick pony that lacks the longevity to compete with top trick-taking games. Rebel Princess and Skull King are recommended as alternatives.
_vid_title: Piñatas Review - Try Not To Be Last One To The Candy!

games.title: Slay the Spire: The Board Game – Downfall
games.opinion: A solid expansion that adds new characters, items, potions, relics, and more content without fundamentally changing the base game experience. Rated 4/5 as an expansion for adding more of the same rather than elevating the game mechanically, but the overall experience remains a 5/5.

games.title: Slay the Spire: The Board Game
games.opinion: An incredible, faithful adaptation of the video game that captures all the magic of the original in a tactile, multiplayer-friendly tabletop form. Originally rated 4.5 but has grown to a 5/5 for the reviewer over time. The only hesitation is that the video game is easier to jump into for quick runs.

games.title: Slay the Spire (video game)
games.opinion: The reviewer has hundreds of hours in the video game and still plays it more than the board game due to its convenience, especially for travel and quick sessions.

games.title: Slay the Spire 2 (video game)
games.opinion: The reviewer has been really enjoying the new characters offered, and notes it now provides multiplayer which was previously a key reason to choose the board game over the video game.

games.title: Downfall (Slay the Spire fan expansion – video game)
games.opinion: The reviewer started playing the digital Downfall fan expansion after playing the board game version, and has enjoyed it as an introduction to the new boss characters.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Game Co reviews Slay the Spire: The Board Game – Downfall, a prototype expansion based on the fan-made Downfall video game mod. The video covers the base board game's strengths as a faithful video game adaptation, introduces the three playable new boss characters (Slime Boss, Guardian, Hexagost) with the Hermit unavailable due to ongoing changes, discusses additional content like new enemies, items, potions, and relics, and shares a personal character ranking. The reviewer rates the expansion 4/5 for adding more content without new mechanics, while the overall game experience remains 5/5. The video also addresses whether fans of the video game or existing board game owners need this expansion.
_vid_title: Slay the Spire: The Board Game - Downfall Review - The Bosses Have Had Enough!

games.title: Brass: Pittsburgh
games.opinion: A great iteration of the Brass series with meaningful new mechanics like oil, localized markets, light/heavy rails, and new buildings. More different from Birmingham than Lancashire was, making it a strong recommendation for hardcore Brass fans and newcomers, though casual Brass players may not need both this and Birmingham.

games.title: Brass: Birmingham
games.opinion: The reviewer's current favorite version of Brass and considers it the best iteration of the engine. Possibly the number one game on Board Game Geek at time of filming. The standard against which Pittsburgh is compared.

games.title: Brass: Lancashire
games.opinion: The reviewer sold their copy after realizing they didn't need multiple versions of Brass given infrequent play. Considered less different from Birmingham than Pittsburgh is.

games.title: Anno 1800
games.opinion: Reviewer's favorite recommendation for fans of Brass. Described as lighter, less mentally draining, thematically similar and mechanically adjacent, also designed by Martin Wallace.

games.title: Food Chain Magnate
games.opinion: Reviewer's favorite Splotter game. Recommended as a heavy, rules-light but strategy-heavy game about finding where you fit in the economic landscape.

games.title: Container
games.opinion: Recommended as a heavy game about synergies between players and buying/selling at the right time. Recently brought back into print by AllPlay.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Game Co reviews Brass: Pittsburgh, the third installment in the Brass series by Roxley Games. The video covers the core mechanics of Brass as a series, then details the changes Pittsburgh introduces including oil as a new resource, localized markets, light and heavy rails replacing canals, new player board buildings like skyscrapers, specialized trains, coke as a resource, and alcohol tokens. The reviewer rates Pittsburgh as a five out of five, praising it as a strong and meaningfully different iteration of the Brass engine, while discussing who should buy it based on their existing familiarity with the series.
_vid_title: Brass: Pittsburgh Review - Everything You Loved About Brass & More!

games.title: Fragart
games.opinion: A charming but limited one-trick pony. Enjoyable push-your-luck set collection gameplay, but lacks strong replayability. Best experienced at a convention or board game cafe rather than purchasing. Compared to downloading an app that entertains for a week before moving on.

games.title: Mayor of Chicago
games.opinion: A highly satisfying and challenging solo puzzle with great diversity of characters and neighborhood-building mechanics. Spalopolis-adjacent but feels distinct. Reviewer lost their first game at higher difficulty which they appreciated. Wants to keep playing it again and again and highly recommends it.

summary: Alex Mackler from Board Gameco reviews and plays through two solo games from Impatience's mini games line: Fragart (designed by Reiner Knizia) and Mayor of Chicago (designed by Xavier Georges). He plays each game on medium-hard difficulty, walks through the gameplay mechanics, and shares his opinions. Fragart receives a 3.5/5 for being fun but limited in replayability, while Mayor of Chicago receives a 4.5/5 as a deeply satisfying and replayable solo challenge.
_vid_title: Freigard & Mayor of Chicago Solo Gameplays & Reviews

games.title: Circle the Wagons: Second Shot
games.opinion: More of the same as the original in a good way. Adds new goals and a fun two-round variant. Not better than the original on its own, but allows for a bigger, more fun session of Circle the Wagons.

games.title: Circle the Wagons
games.opinion: A huge fan of the original. Describes it as fantastic, fun, travel-friendly, affordable, and easy to play. One of their favorite games from Buttonshy. Rates it 4.5 out of 5.

games.title: Sprawlopolis
games.opinion: Preferred for solo play over Circle the Wagons solo mode. Similar card overlaying and goal structures. Favorite version among the Polis series.

games.title: Skulls of Sedlec
games.opinion: Described as their favorite two-player card-based game from Buttonshy, with a similar structure but very different gameplay. Highly recommended.

summary: Alex Wackle from Board Game Co. reviews Circle the Wagons: Second Shot from Buttonshy Games, a two-player drafting card game. He demonstrates gameplay, explains scoring, and discusses how Second Shot adds new goals and a two-round variant to the original Circle the Wagons. He rates it 4.5 out of 5 and recommends Skulls of Sedlec and Sprawlopolis as related games.
_vid_title: Circle the Wagons 2nd Shot Review - More of the Same, In A Good Way!

games.title: Burgle Bros 3: Future Flip
games.opinion: A solid solo and cooperative game with good tension, strategy, and replayability. Slightly above Burgle Bros 2 in the reviewer's estimation. Minor complaints about occasional repetitive busy work and susceptibility to alpha gaming. Enjoys the balance of prediction, risk assessment, and chaos management.

games.title: Burgle Bros 2
games.opinion: A previous entry in the series that the reviewer has played extensively. Burgle Bros 3 is rated slightly above it.

games.title: Pandemic
games.opinion: Mentioned as a comparable cooperative game experience, noting similar moments of waiting for the right cards.

games.title: The Loop
games.opinion: Mentioned as having similar cooperative vibes to Burgle Bros 3, though mechanically different.

games.title: Alibis
games.opinion: Recommended as a sponsored title from Allplay. Cooperative but mechanically different, focused on matching clues and giving hints to teammates.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Game Co reviews Burgle Bros 3: Future Flip by Fowers Games, a 1-4 player cooperative stealth game. The video includes a brief how-to-play demonstration followed by a review covering what the reviewer likes (tension, strategy, abilities, replayability), dislikes (occasional busy work waiting for cards), and potential concerns for others (bad luck moments, alpha gaming). The game receives a 4 out of 5 rating. Pandemic, The Loop, and Alibis are recommended as related games.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: Burgle Bros 3: Future Flip Review - Schmooze Up Those Coworkers

games.title: Shallow Sea
games.opinion: A highly enjoyable tile-laying optimization game that the reviewer genuinely loves, though it feels too heavily inspired by Cascadia and runs longer than the stated 30-45 minute play time. The challenge of balancing fish, coral, and sea life keeps them coming back for more.

games.title: Shallow Sea: Nesting Season
games.opinion: A smooth but minor expansion that adds the nesting season board, new goals, turtles, and additional tiles. Integrates naturally but doesn't dramatically change the experience. Some design choices like only two turtles and minimally different variable scoring tiles are seen as misses.

games.title: Cascadia
games.opinion: Highly praised as a phenomenal game that Shallow Sea draws heavily from, perhaps too heavily. The reviewer loves the Cascadia system and recommends it as a companion game.

games.title: Cascadia: Alpine Lakes
games.opinion: Recommended alongside Cascadia as another phenomenal version of the system.

games.title: Harmonies
games.opinion: Briefly mentioned as an example of a game that feels similarly inspired by Cascadia but remains distinct enough not to feel like a copy.

games.title: Wondrous Creatures
games.opinion: Recommended as another game from Bad Comic Games that the reviewer thoroughly enjoys, praised for its satisfying card comboing.

games.title: Keystone: North America
games.opinion: Recommended as a challenging nature-themed board optimization game that the reviewer finds incredibly satisfying, suggested for fans of similar games.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Gameco reviews the tile-laying game Shallow Sea and its expansion Nesting Season from Bad Comic Games. The video covers how to play the base game, explains the expansion's additions including the nesting season board and new goals, and provides a detailed review highlighting the game's satisfying optimization challenge, its Cascadia-adjacent design, longer-than-advertised play time, and an overall score of 4.5 out of 5.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: Shallow Sea & Nesting Season Expansion Review - Delightfully Challenging

games.title: Illiterati
games.opinion: A solid cooperative word-building game that the reviewer enjoys, rating it 4 out of 5 as a base game. Described as cooperative scrabble-like gameplay with restrictions and enemy attacks.

games.title: Illiterati: Red Herrings & White Roses
games.opinion: A decent expansion with some enjoyable elements like new book variety, but the Unwritten and Allies felt like more of the same with added rules overhead. The new book types were highlights, but the new antagonists and ally mechanics didn't meaningfully enhance the experience. Recommended for big fans of the base game, but skippable for casual players.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Game Co reviews the first expansion for Illiterati, Red Herrings & White Roses, covering its new elements: the Unwritten antagonists, the Literacy Meter, new Ally cards, and new Green and Pink book categories. The reviewer rates the expansion 3.5 out of 5, praising the new book varieties but finding the Unwritten and Allies added more rules complexity than meaningful gameplay enhancement.
_vid_title: ILLITERATI: Red Herrings & White Roses Review - The Unwritten Have Arrived!

games.title: Pandemic Legacy Season 1
games.opinion: Favorite legacy game overall, first legacy game played, colored by nostalgia but still considered the best Pandemic Legacy and best legacy game. Gives challenge, teamwork, twists, well-done difficulty arc, and ruined regular Pandemic for the reviewer.

games.title: Ticket to Ride Legacy: Legends of the West
games.opinion: First game since Pandemic Legacy Season 1 to give the highs of what a legacy game can be. Rewarding, engaging, and highly recommended for both Ticket to Ride fans and legacy game fans. Has a second copy ready to play again.

games.title: My City
games.opinion: Played more than any other game ever — 10 full campaigns (240 sessions). Simple, fun, accessible, good progression. Only ranked 3rd because familiarity is finally causing fatigue. Arguably could be number one.

games.title: Pandemic Legacy Season 2
games.opinion: Excellent legacy experience, very close to Season 1 in quality. Played twice and would go back for a third time. Main complaint is it felt slightly worse than Season 1, but only a small drop-off.

games.title: My City: Roll & Build
games.opinion: Delightful and charming, slightly different from My City with elements liked more and some less. Simple, accessible fun across 12 missions. Manages to be distinct yet familiar.

games.title: Rise of Queensdale
games.opinion: Felt like a better version of Charterstone. Enjoyable enough that the reviewer hunted down a copy to play again. Details are fuzzy from memory but remembers it fondly.

games.title: Sagrada Artisans
games.opinion: Really enjoyable. Builds on Sagrada with unlocking, coloring, and new mechanics. Didn't drastically change the base game but gave nice twists. Recommended for fans of Sagrada, legacy games, or coloring.

games.title: Kingdom Legacy
games.opinion: Surprisingly fun solo card game. Stupid simple fun, great travel game, charming and addicting. Downside is poor AI artwork. Enjoyable for the experience rather than winning or losing.

games.title: Pandemic Legacy Season 0
games.opinion: Least favorite of the Pandemic Legacy series but still fun. Felt like ending the series on a low point compared to Seasons 1 and 2. Wants to replay it with better-adjusted expectations.

games.title: Charterstone
games.opinion: Fun worker placement game played twice, could see a third playthrough. Issues with player count scaling and tension between unlocking things vs. winning. Charming art and story, though story is a little weird.

games.title: Machi Koro Legacy
games.opinion: Simple, straightforward, and charming. Didn't wow with unlocks but surprises felt more genuine than Aeon's End Legacy. Would play again and daughter has requested another playthrough.

games.title: Clank! Legacy 2: Acquisitions Incorporated
games.opinion: Slightly better than Clank Legacy 1 due to finishing it and playing with more invested players, but same core complaints about mid-game stickering and interruptions remain. Built on the bones of the first.

games.title: Jurassic World Legacy of Isla Nublar
games.opinion: Recommended at two players, not three or four. Gets overwhelmed at higher player counts due to limited actions. Enjoyable overall with a nice chunk of unlocks at the midpoint, though some parts felt uninteresting.

games.title: My Island
games.opinion: Disappointment compared to My City. Played 16 of 24 missions before stopping. Not bad but got too fiddly and felt like it was cramming in mechanics. Would rather replay My City.

games.title: Aeon's End Legacy
games.opinion: Fine deck-builder with a legacy layer. Played twice but never wowed by the legacy elements. Felt like stripped-down Aeon's End slowly rebuilt. No wow moments and least impactful legacy changes among the games.

games.title: Clank! Legacy: Acquisitions Incorporated
games.opinion: Well-loved by others but only got halfway through due to group disinterest. Not bad, just not compelling enough to drive continued play. Mid-game stickering constantly interrupted the experience.

games.title: Wild Tales
games.opinion: Forgettable pirate legacy game from Buttonshhy Games. Felt fiddly with unclear rules and unrewarding experience. Kudos for fitting legacy in 18 cards but the result was not enjoyable. Short enough to finish, ranked above Divinous for that reason.

games.title: Divinous
games.opinion: Least favorite on the list. Did not finish because interest faded. Not a bad game but never delivered enough to justify the time investment. Resented it more because of how long it took.

games.title: Zombie Kids Evolution
games.opinion: Kid-focused legacy game. Did a great job introducing children to legacy games. Would roughly place around 19th-20th if included. Not ranked due to different target audience.

games.title: Zombie Teens Evolution
games.opinion: Kid-focused legacy game. Similar to Zombie Kids, great for introducing children to legacy games. Would roughly place around 19th-20th if included. Not ranked due to different target audience.

games.title: Star Realms: Rise of an Empire
games.opinion: Currently three games in. Not ranked because the full experience has not been seen yet.

games.title: Flashpoint Legacy: Flame of Disaster
games.opinion: Played first three sessions. Not ranked because the full experience has not been completed.

games.title: Sail Legacy
games.opinion: Played 10 games but not yet at the halfway point of the full experience. Not ranked as it is not a complete experience yet.

games.title: The Witcher Legacy
games.opinion: Upcoming crowdfunding campaign. Cannot give a full opinion yet. Not ranked.

summary: Alex Rackle from Board Gameco ranks all 18 legacy games he has completed, from least to most favorite. He defines legacy games as those requiring permanent destruction or alteration of components, excluding campaign games. He also provides notes on games he started but didn't finish, games he hasn't fully completed, and kid-focused titles excluded from the main ranking. The ranked list runs from Divinous (18th) up to Pandemic Legacy Season 1 (1st), with commentary on each game's strengths, weaknesses, and personal enjoyment.
_vid_title: Ranking Every Legacy Game I've Ever Played
